content.full_text:
Re
Fern PolrrrcAI- PRACTTcES LoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax t9l6) 322-0886
January 29,2008
Jack L. White
City Attorney
City of Anaheim
200 S. Anaheim Blvd., Suite 356
Anaheim, California 92805
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-07-197
Dear Mr. White:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tom Kupfrian and
Phyllis Mueller regarding the contlict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").' Pleare note that we lack specific infbrmation as to the govemmental
decisions the public officials will be making and, therefore, provide only informal
assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity
provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 84113; Regulation
18329(cX3).) Our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer
no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common
law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Also, the Commission will
not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) Therefore,
nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already
taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective
actions. Finally, some of your questions, particularly those relating to the role of a
Neighborhood Development Coordinator yet to he hired, are purely hypothetical. We are
declining to issue assistance as to these questions at this time. (Regulation
I 8329(bX8)(D), copy enclosed.)
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections ltt I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. l-07-197
Page No. 2
QUESTTONS
1. May the Historic Preservation Coordinator participate in making govemmental
decisions that impact the Historic District, including decisions involving individual
properties within the Historic District, if he owns one property within the Historic District
and another within 500 feet of the Historic District, or if he divests himself of his interest
in the real property within the Historic District?
2. Does the joint ownership of property by the former Neighborhood
Development Coordinator and the Historic Preservation Coordinator create a partnership
interest to be evaluated in determining potential conflicts of interest?
3. May the recently retired Neighborhood Development Coordinator apply for
and enter into a Mills Act agreement with the City of Anaheim for her residence located
in the Historic District?
CONCLUSIONS
l. No. He may only participate in these decisions if it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the decisions will have any financial impact on his economic interests, as
discussed below.
2. Yes. When two or more individuals own real property used for rental
purposes, this joint venture is a "business entity" under the Act. As discussed below, this
business entity is a separate economic interest for purposes of conflict-of-interest
analysis.
3. Yes. Nothing in the Act bars the former Neighborhood Development
Coordinator from applying for a Mills Act agreement for her property as she is no longer
a public official.
FACTS
The City of Anaheim generally employs a Neighborhood Development
Coordinator and a Historic Preservation Coordinator in the Community Development
Department to administer and implement the City's Historic District programs and Mills
Act program. Phyllis Mueller, the former Neighborhood Development Coordinator,
recently retired, and it is expected that a replacement will be hired. Tom Kupfrian, the
Historic Preservation Coordinator, remains employed by the City.
The Mills Act is a state law allowing cities to enter into agreements with owners
of historic buildings to have their property taxes calculated in an alternative manner in
exchange for maintaining and, if necessary, restoring the buildings. Anaheim has
approved the use of Mills Act agreements with owners of qualifying properties in
designated historic districts (collectively the "Historic District"). The Community


File No. I-07-197
Page No. 3
Development Department is also analyzing expansion of the Mills Act program to
include homes outside the Historic District. Inclusion in the Historic District is believed
to significantly increase the value of property and to cause the property to be less
susceptible to decreases in value in a weak market.
Both a Neighborhood Development Coordinator and the Historic Preservation
Coordinator participate in government decisions regarding specific properties in the
Historic District, including evaluating properties for eligibility for Mills Act agreements
and determining if properties subject to the agreements are in compliance. They also
participate in more general decisions regarding the Historic District as a whole.
Mr. Kupfrian owns a personal residence located within 500 feet of the Historic
District that may be included in the Mills Act program if the program is expanded. He
also owns jointly with Ms. Mueller a residence operated as income property that is
subject to a Mills Act agreement in the Historic District.
Ms. Mueller also owns two other properties within the Historic District, an
income property and her residence. She has filed an application to enter her personal
residence into a Mills Act agreement.
AI\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Ms. Mueller
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to "public officials." A public
official means "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency." Unless Ms. Mueller occupies some other position within this
definition, she is no longer a public official now that her employment has ceased. Once a
public official has left public service, the conflict-of-interest rules are no longer
applicable. While there are certain restrictions on public officials who leave government
service, (Section 87406.3), the Act limits these restrictions, as they apply to former
employees of local govemment agencies, to local elected officials, chief administrative
officers of a county, city managers, or general managers or chief administrators of a
special district. Accordingly, the Act does not impose any restrictions on Ms. Mueller or
prohibit or restrict her from applying for or entering into a Mills Act agreement with the
City of Anaheim. Please note, however, that local ethics rules may apply.


File No. l-07-197
Page No.4
Mr. Kupfrian
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.
Step 1. Is Mr. Kupfrian a "public official" within the meaning of Section
87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local govemment agency." As an employee of the City of
Anaheim, which is a local agency, Mr. Kupfrian is a public official. Therefore, he may
not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his position to influence any decisions
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of his economic
interests.
Step 2. Will Mr. Kupfrian be making, participating in making or influencing
a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreementonbehalf of hisorheragency. (Regulation 18702.1.) Apublicofficial
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the govemmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, ffiy
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) In
participating in general decisions regarding the Historic District as a whole and decisions
regarding specific properties in the Historic District, including evaluating properties for
eligibility for Mills Act agreements, and determining if specific properties subject to the
agreements are in compliance, Mr. Kupfrian would be participating in making
governmental decisions.


File No. I-07-197
Page No. 5
Step 3. What are Mr. Kupfrian's economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in
Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
Regulation I 8703. 1(a).)
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 1 8703. 1(b).)
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 8703.3.)
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)
Mr. Kupfrian has an economic interest in his personal residence and the income
property he owns jointly with Ms. Mueller, assuming he has an investment of $2,000 or
more in each property. He also has an economic interest in any tenants of the income
property from whom he receives $500 or more in a year as sources of income. Finally,
this income property also gives rise to an economic interest in a business entity. We have
previously advised that when two or more individuals own real property for the purpose
of making a profit, this is a joint venture2 recognized as a "business entity" under the Act.
(Daniel Advice Letter, No. A-00-134;Whitson Advice Letter, No. A-98-148.)
2 The common definition of a 'Joint venture" is a "special combination of two or more persons,
where, in some specific venture, a protit is jointly sought without any actual partnership or corporate
designation, or as an association ofpersons to carry out a single business enterprise for profit, for which
purpose they combine their property, money, effects, skill and knowledge." (Sadugor v. Holstein (1962)
199 Cal. App. 2d 477,483.)


File No. I-07-197
Page No. 6
Step 4. Will Mr. Kupfrian's economic interests be directly or indirectly
involved in decisions he will make, participate in making or influence as a public
official?
The governmental decisions that Mr. Kupfrian will face are decisions involving
the Historic District or property within the Historic District.
Real Property Interests. Regulation 18704.2(a)(l) provides that real property in
which a public official has an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental
decision if the real property, or any part of the real property, is located within 500 feet of
the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property that is the subject of the
governmental decision. As to decisions involving the Historic District generally, all of
the Historic District would be involved. Hence, both properties in which Mr. Kupfrian
has an interest would be directly involved because one is located within the Historic
District while the other is located within 500 feet of the Historic District. As to decisions
involving any particular property within the Historic District, unless the properties in
which he has an interest are located within 500 feet of the subject property,
Mr. Kupfrian's properties would not be directly involved in these decisions. Of course, if
the decision involves his own property, the property would be directly involved. (This
could occur if, for example, Mr. Kupfrian were to be faced with a decision to determine if
his property is in compliance with the terms of the Mills Act agreement.) Real property
that is not directly involved in a governmental decision is deemed to be indirectly
involved. (Regulation 187 04.2(d)(2).)
Business Entities and Sources of Income. Under Regulation 18704.1(a) a person,
including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person."
Lacking specific information about the decisions in which Mr. Kupfrian wishes to
participate, we cannot determine whether his business entity or sources of income will be
directly or indirectly involved in these decisions. Business entities and sources of income
that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are
regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) Accordingly, our further


File No. l-07-197
Page No. 7
analysis is limited to Mr. Kupfrian's real property economic interest. Please feel free to
seek further advice, if necessary, with respect to any future governmental decisions once
those decisions become clear.
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the agency's decision.
Regulation 18705.2 sets forth the materiality standard for economic interests in
real property and provides, in subdivision (a)(1), that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on directly involved real property is presumed to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect, not even one penny, on the real
property.
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
governmental decisions on Mr. Kupfrian's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. (In re Thorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.
Because both of Mr. Kupfrian's properties would be directly involved in decisions
involving the Historic District generally, the financial effect of these governmental
decisions on his properties is presumed to be material.
Step 7. Despite a determination that Mr. Kupfrian has a potential conflict of
interest, may he participate in a decision because the financial effect on his interest
is not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally?
The "public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision
despite a conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation
18707.) In other words, the exception woUld apply if the decision will affect a significant


File No. l-07-197
Page No. 8
segment of the public in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public
official's interests.
Regulation 18707(b) sets forth a four-step process to determine whether the effect
of a decision is distinguishable (or not) from the effect on the public generally:
"(l) Step One: Identify each specific person or real property
(economic interest) that is materially affected by the governmental
decision.
"(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step
One, determine the applicable 'significant segment' rule according to the
provisions of [Regulation 18707. l(b)].
"(3) Step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected
by the governmental decision as set forth in the applicable 'significant
segment' rule. If the answer is 'no,' then the analysis ends because the
first prong of a two-parr test set forth in [Regulation 1g707.1(b)] is not
met, and the public official cannot participate in the governmental
decision. If the answer is 'yes,' proceed to Step Four.
"(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of [Regulation
18707 .1(bX2)1, determine if the person or real property identified in Step
One is affected by the governmental decision in 'substantially the same
manner' as other persons or real property in the applicable significant
segment. If the answer is 'yes' as to each person or real property
identified in Step One, then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally and the public official may
participate in the decision. If the answer is 'no' as to any person or real
property identified in Step One, the public official may not participate in
the governmental decision unless one of the special rules sei forthln
[Regulations 18707.2 through 18707.9] applies to each person or real
property triggering the conflict of interest." (Regulation 1g707(b)(l-4).)
Regulation 18707.1(b)(1XB), regarding real property, aids in determining whether
the govemmental decision affects a significant segment of the population. Under that
regulation, the decision must also affect ten percent or more of ali property owners or all
residential property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's ug"n"y br the district the
official represents, or 5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency. (Regularion lg707 .1(B)(i)-(ii).)
You stated that the Historic District has fewer than 5,000 homes and that the City
of Anaheim has approximately l0l,5l0 housing units. With the facts you provided, it is
unstated, but doubtftrl that this comprises ten percent of the property o*n.is in the


File No. l-O7-197
Page No. 9
District. This prong is likely not satisfied with respect to decisions that affect the Historic
District generally or particular property within the Historic District.
step 8. If Mr. Kupfrian has a conflict of interest in a decision, may he
nevertheless participate because his participation is legally required?
Section 87101 (Regulation 18708) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a govemmental decision may nevertheless
participate. You have not provided facts indicating that this exception is applicable to
Mr. Kupfrian's situation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
%c,ng&/*
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
VJjgl
Enclosure

File No. l-07-197
analysis is limited to Mr. Kupfrian's real property economic interest. Please feel free to

real property and provides, in subdivision (a)(1), that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on directly involved real property is presumed to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect, not even one penny, on the real
property.
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
governmental decisions on Mr. Kupfrian's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. (In re Thorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.
Because both of Mr. Kupfrian's properties would be directly involved in decisions
involving the Historic District generally, the financial effect of these governmental
decisions on his properties is presumed to be material.
Step 7. Despite a determination that Mr. Kupfrian has a potential conflict of
interest, may he participate in a decision because the financial effect on his interest
is not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally?
The "public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision
despite a conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation
18707.) In other words, the exception woUld apply if the decision will affect a significant


File No. l-07-197
segment of the public in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public
official's interests.
Regulation 18707(b) sets forth a four-step process to determine whether the effect
of a decision is distinguishable (or not) from the effect on the public generally:
"(l) Step One: Identify each specific person or real property
(economic interest) that is materially affected by the governmental
decision.
"(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step
One, determine the applicable 'significant segment' rule according to the
provisions of [Regulation 18707. l(b)].
"(3) Step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected
by the governmental decision as set forth in the applicable 'significant
segment' rule. If the answer is 'no,' then the analysis ends because the
first prong of a two-parr test set forth in [Regulation 1g707.1(b)] is not
met, and the public official cannot participate in the governmental
decision. If the answer is 'yes,' proceed to Step Four.
"(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of [Regulation
18707 .1(bX2)1, determine if the person or real property identified in Step
One is affected by the governmental decision in 'substantially the same
manner' as other persons or real property in the applicable significant
segment. If the answer is 'yes' as to each person or real property
identified in Step One, then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally and the public official may
participate in the decision. If the answer is 'no' as to any person or real
property identified in Step One, the public official may not participate in
the governmental decision unless one of the special rules sei forthln
[Regulations 18707.2 through 18707.9] applies to each person or real
property triggering the conflict of interest." (Regulation 1g707(b)(l-4).)
Regulation 18707.1(b)(1XB), regarding real property, aids in determining whether
the govemmental decision affects a significant segment of the population. Under that
regulation, the decision must also affect ten percent or more of ali property owners or all
residential property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's ug"n"y br the district the
official represents, or 5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency. (Regularion lg707 .1(B)(i)-(ii).)
You stated that the Historic District has fewer than 5,000 homes and that the City
of Anaheim has approximately l0l,5l0 housing units. With the facts you provided, it is
unstated, but doubtftrl that this comprises ten percent of the property o*n.is in the


File No. l-O7-197
District. This prong is likely not satisfied with respect to decisions that affect the Historic
District generally or particular property within the Historic District.
step 8. If Mr. Kupfrian has a conflict of interest in a decision, may he
nevertheless participate because his participation is legally required?
Section 87101 (Regulation 18708) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a govemmental decision may nevertheless
participate. You have not provided facts indicating that this exception is applicable to
Mr. Kupfrian's situation.

.
3. Yes. Nothing in the Act bars the former Neighborhood Development
Coordinator from applying for a Mills Act agreement for her property as she is no longer
a public official.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May the Historic Preservation Coordinator participate in making govemmental
decisions that impact the Historic District, including decisions involving individual
properties within the Historic District, if he owns one property within the Historic District
and another within 500 feet of the Historic District, or if he divests himself of his interest
in the real property within the Historic District?
2. Does the joint ownership of property by the former Neighborhood
Development Coordinator and the Historic Preservation Coordinator create a partnership
interest to be evaluated in determining potential conflicts of interest?
3. May the recently retired Neighborhood Development Coordinator apply for
and enter into a Mills Act agreement with the City of Anaheim for her residence located
in the Historic District?

CONCLUSION: l. No. He may only participate in these decisions if it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the decisions will have any financial impact on his economic interests, as
discussed below.
2. Yes. When two or more individuals own real property used for rental
purposes, this joint venture is a "business entity" under the Act. As discussed below, this
business entity is a separate economic interest for purposes of conflict-of-interest

White City Attorney City of Anaheim 200 S. Anaheim Blvd., Suite 356 Anaheim, California 92805 Your Request for Advice Our File No. I-07-197 Dear Mr. White: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tom Kupfrian and Phyllis Mueller regarding the contlict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Pleare note that we lack specific infbrmation as to the govemmental decisions the public officials will be making and, therefore, provide only informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 84113; Regulation 18329(cX3).) Our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Also, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. Finally, some of your questions, particularly those relating to the role of a Neighborhood Development Coordinator yet to he hired, are purely hypothetical. We are declining to issue assistance as to these questions at this time. (Regulation I 8329(bX8)(D), copy enclosed.) ' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections ltt I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations. May the Historic Preservation Coordinator participate in making govemmental decisions that impact the Historic District, including decisions involving individual properties within the Historic District, if he owns one property within the Historic District and another within 500 feet of the Historic District, or if he divests himself of his interest in the real property within the Historic District? 2. Does the joint ownership of property by the former Neighborhood Development Coordinator and the Historic Preservation Coordinator create a partnership interest to be evaluated in determining potential conflicts of interest? 3. May the recently retired Neighborhood Development Coordinator apply for and enter into a Mills Act agreement with the City of Anaheim for her residence located in the Historic District?
